principles.statement: Use the right Copilot primitive for the job: instructions for ambient context, agents for role and tool boundaries, skills for step-by-step methodology, prompts for thin reusable entrypoints, and hooks for deterministic lifecycle automation.

Keep always-on instructions (copilot-instructions.md) short, factual, and narrowly scoped. Every token of always-on content is injected into every prompt, so verbosity creates token drag and dilutes important rules.
Include reasoning alongside rules in instructions. A rule that explains 'why' is more durable than one that just states 'what' — models follow instructions better when they understand the intent.
Focus instructions on non-obvious rules and project-specific conventions. Do not restate language fundamentals or common best practices the model already knows.
Use file-based .instructions.md with applyTo globs for language-specific or directory-specific rules instead of bloating the always-on copilot-instructions.md with conditionals.
Instruction priority flows Personal > Repository > Organization. If you need to override org-wide rules for a specific repo or user, this is the built-in mechanism.
Constrain agents with narrow roles, least-privilege tool access, and explicit stopping rules. A well-defined agent knows what it should NOT do as clearly as what it should do.
Use agent handoffs for sequential multi-phase workflows. Define handoff label, target agent, prompt, and the send field to pass conversation context or a summary between phases.
The tools field in .agent.md defines a maximum set. List only what the agent should use — do not give agents blanket access to all tools when they only need a focused subset.

Tool list priority: tools specified in a prompt file override the agent's tool list, which overrides the default. Design accordingly — a prompt can narrow an agent's capabilities for a specific entrypoint.

evidenceRefs.title: VS Code — Prompt files (tool list priority)

principles.statement: Move step-by-step methodology into skills (SKILL.md), not into agents or instructions. Skills use progressive loading (discovery→instructions→resources) so methodology is loaded only when needed.

Skill names and descriptions are the discovery surface. The model chooses skills based on these fields matching the user's apparent intent. Write descriptions that clearly state when to use and when not to use the skill.

Bundle skill resources (reference docs, templates, examples) as files alongside the SKILL.md. The skill's instructions file can reference paths relative to the skill folder for the agent to read at execution time.
Prompt files (.prompt.md) should be thin manual entrypoints, not full playbooks. If a prompt has complex multi-step logic, extract it into a skill and reference the skill from the agent.

Use hooks for deterministic automation, not guidance. Unlike instructions that guide behavior, hooks execute shell commands with guaranteed outcomes. Use them for formatting, linting, security policies, logging, and approval gates.

Design agents with explicit phases and stopping rules. Production agents (e.g., desktop/desktop's deskocat) use numbered phases (Understand→Plan→Implement→Verify→Deliver) with clear exit conditions at each phase.

Judge customization quality by goals, boundaries, inputs, and verification conditions — not by tone, length, or apparent confidence. A concise instruction with clear scope beats a verbose one that sounds authoritative.

Always verify YAML frontmatter syntax before judging content quality. Missing the opening '---' fence, using wrong field names (e.g., 'mode' instead of 'agent' in .prompt.md), or misspelling fields silently disables metadata.

Share always-on instructions across agents by referencing them from the agents list in copilot-instructions.md or via the AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md format. Avoid duplicating the same guidance across multiple agents.
Use subagents for context isolation when delegating multi-step tasks. Each subagent runs with its own context window, preventing long-running workflows from exceeding the main agent's context budget.
Use the 'agent' field (not deprecated 'mode') in .prompt.md frontmatter to route a prompt to a specific agent. Using the deprecated field silently prevents the slash command from registering.

Hooks receive structured JSON via stdin and return JSON via stdout. Exit code 0 means success (parse stdout), exit code 2 means blocking error (stop and show error), any other code means non-blocking warning (continue).
When multiple hooks decisions conflict, the most restrictive wins. A PreToolUse hook returning 'deny' overrides another returning 'allow'. Design hooks defensively knowing this priority rule.

Use public examples to extract durable principles, not to copy prose verbatim. No single repository is the platform standard — patterns that recur across multiple real repos are more trustworthy than any one implementation.

Validate customization files incrementally before judging the whole setup. Check file placement, then frontmatter syntax, then field values, then content quality. A well-written instruction in the wrong location does nothing.

Write skill and agent descriptions as discovery surfaces with the same rigor as API tool descriptions: state what the component does, when to invoke it, when NOT to invoke it, and the shape of its output. Vague descriptions cause mis-routing and missed invocations.

evidenceRefs.title: Anthropic — Advanced tool use (Tool Search Tool)

principles.statement: Use progressive loading to keep context lean — skills should expose only name and description during routing, deferring full methodology until invocation. This mirrors the defer_loading pattern: load definitions only when actually needed to preserve context budget.

evidenceRefs.title: Anthropic — Advanced tool use (Tool Search Tool, defer_loading)

principles.statement: Delegate multi-step workflows to subagents for context isolation. Each subagent runs in its own context window, preventing intermediate results from polluting the main agent's context — the same principle as programmatic tool calling where only final output enters the model's context.

Include concrete invocation examples in skills and agent descriptions. Anthropic's testing showed tool use examples improved parameter accuracy from 72% to 90%. Skills that demonstrate realistic inputs and expected outputs are more reliably selected and correctly invoked.

evidenceRefs.title: Anthropic — Advanced tool use (Tool Use Examples)
